matched_cl_name: United States v. Bowie, Juan
gold_cite: United States v. Moore, 651 F.3d 30 (2011)
proposition: “When evidence of such acts is intrinsic to the charged crime, it is not evidence of other acts and is thus wholly unregulated by Rule 404(b).” (cleaned up)
original_rationale: Bowie actually criticizes and rejects the broad 'intrinsic acts' exception to Rule 404(b), holding the rule is not bypassed merely because evidence is inextricably intertwined with the charged crime.
new_rationale: Bowie criticizes the intrinsic/inextricably intertwined doctrine as serving little function and rejects broad exceptions to Rule 404(b), rather than endorsing the proposition that intrinsic acts are wholly unregulated by 404(b).

matched_cl_name: Grant v. Raymond
gold_cite: Shaw v. Cooper, 32 U.S. 292 (1833)
proposition: “The policy of granting exclusive privileges in certain cases, was deemed of so much importance in a national point of view, that power was given to congress in the federal constitution.”
original_rationale: The excerpt addresses patent surrender/reissue procedure and the Secretary of State's authority, not the constitutional policy rationale for granting Congress power over exclusive privileges.
new_rationale: Marshall's opinion notes that promoting useful arts is a constitutional aim and quotes the patent/copyright clause, supporting the general idea that exclusive privileges were deemed important enough to be given to Congress, but the case does not frame this as a holding about the policy of granting exclusive privileges per se.

matched_cl_name: Tison v. Arizona
gold_cite: McMillan v. Gomez, 19 F.3d 465 (1994)
proposition: “The felony murder rule is too deeply rooted in Anglo-American jurisprudence to be questioned now.”
original_rationale: Tison addresses Eighth Amendment proportionality of the death penalty for non-triggerman felony-murder accomplices, not the historical entrenchment of the felony murder rule itself.
new_rationale: Tison addresses Eighth Amendment proportionality of the death penalty for non-triggerman felony murderers and does not declare the felony murder rule itself beyond question.

matched_cl_name: Ragansky v. United States
gold_cite: Postell v. United States, 282 A.2d 551 (1971)
proposition: “[T]he mere fact that the infliction of the harm is upon condition does not by any means preclude it from being a threat within the meaning of the Code.”
original_rationale: The excerpt addresses whether a threat made in jest is punishable, not whether a conditional threat qualifies as a threat under the statute.
new_rationale: opinion fits within 20K, no new text exposed

matched_cl_name: Old Chief v. United States
gold_cite: United States v. Han, 962 F.3d 568 (2020)
proposition: appropriate safeguards to minimize the danger of unfair prejudice include a limit on what details the Government can elicit and a precautionary limiting instruction
original_rationale: The excerpt addresses Rule 403 balancing and stipulation to a prior conviction; it does not discuss limiting the Government's elicited details plus a precautionary limiting instruction as safeguards.
new_rationale: Old Chief addresses Rule 403 exclusion via stipulation for felon-status elements, not safeguards like limiting Government's elicitation details and giving precautionary limiting instructions to cure unfair prejudice.

matched_cl_name: Donald F. Greeno v. George Daley
gold_cite: Sain v. Wood, 512 F.3d 886 (2008)
proposition: a medical professional is “entitled to deference in treatment decisions unless ‘no minimally competent medical professional would have so responded under these circumstances’ ” (quoting Collignon v. Milwaukee County, 163 F.3d 982, 988 (7th Cir.1998)
original_rationale: The Greeno excerpt does not contain the quoted Collignon language about deference to medical professionals; the proposition's quoted standard is not articulated in this excerpt.
new_rationale: The Greeno opinion does not contain the quoted Collignon language about deference to medical professionals' treatment decisions; that phrase appears in other Seventh Circuit cases but not in this excerpt.

matched_cl_name: Lexmark Int'l, Inc. v. Static Control Components, Inc.
gold_cite: Certified Pub. Accts. v. I.R.S., 804 F.3d 1193 (2015)
proposition: explaining that the zone of interests test must be treated procedurally like any other “non- jurisdictional issue”
original_rationale: Lexmark reframes the zone-of-interests inquiry as statutory interpretation, not as guidance on the procedural treatment of non-jurisdictional issues.
new_rationale: Lexmark holds the zone-of-interests test is a statutory cause-of-action question, not jurisdictional, but it does not frame the test as a 'non-jurisdictional issue' to be treated procedurally like any other such issue.

matched_cl_name: Hausler v. JP Morgan Chase Bank, N.A.
proposition: holding that “[b]y its terms, [TRIA] § 201 does not provide for execution against assets that are not blocked”
original_rationale: Hausler addresses whether mid-stream EFTs are attachable property of Cuba under TRIA Â§ 201, not whether Â§ 201 limits execution to blocked assets only; the case concerns blocked EFTs throughout.
new_rationale: opinion fits within 20K, no new text exposed

matched_cl_name: Nautilus, Inc. v. Biosig Instruments, Inc.

gold_cite: Festo Corp. v. Shoketsu Kinzoku Kogyo Kabushiki Co., 535 U.S. 722 (2014)
proposition: explaining that the definiteness “requirement is not a demand for unreasonable precision” and that “verbal precision” in every case is not possible “in a patent system that actually works, in practice”
original_rationale: Nautilus articulates a 'reasonable certainty' standard and does not contain the quoted language about 'unreasonable precision' or 'verbal precision' in 'a patent system that actually works'â€”that phrasing comes from other cases.
new_rationale: Nautilus acknowledges language's inherent limitations and that absolute precision is unattainable, supporting the general idea, but the specific quoted phrases about 'unreasonable precision' and 'verbal precision...in a patent system that actually works' do not appear in this excerpt.

matched_cl_name: Dawson Chemical Co. v. Rohm & Haas Co.
gold_cite: Acumed LLC v. Stryker Corp., 551 F.3d 1323 (2008)
proposition: “The essential attribute of a patent grant is that it provides a right to exclude competitors from infringing the patent”
original_rationale: The excerpt addresses contributory infringement and patent misuse under 35 U.S.C. Â§ 271, not the general proposition that the essential attribute of a patent is the right to exclude competitors.
new_rationale: The opinion discusses the right to exclude as central to patent grants (e.g., quoting Carbice on the patentee's monopoly and discussing § 271(d)'s 'limited power to exclude'), but the case's main holding concerns patent misuse and contributory infringement, not a direct articulation of the proposition.

gold_cite: Williams v. Howard Univ., 984 F. Supp. 27 (1997)

matched_cl_name: Atwater v. City of Lago Vista
gold_name: Atwater v. City of Lago Vista
gold_cite: Atwater v. City of Lago Vista, 532 U.S. 318 (2001)
proposition: reserving the question of whether “the Fourth Amendment entails an ‘in the presence’ requirement for purposes of misdemeanor arrests”
original_rationale: Atwater addressed whether warrantless arrests for fine-only misdemeanors violate the Fourth Amendment and did not reserve the 'in the presence' question; the excerpt shows the Court resolving the case on probable cause grounds without reserving that issue.
new_rationale: The Court squarely held that warrantless arrests for misdemeanors not committed in the officer's presence are permissible under the Fourth Amendment in this case, but the opinion does not reserve or address whether an 'in the presence' requirement exists—Atwater's offense was committed in the officer's presence, so the issue was not even raised.

matched_cl_name: Atwater v. City of Lago Vista
gold_cite: United States v. Gonzalez, 107 F.4th 1304 (2024)
proposition: noting that every Circuit to face the issue has held that neither a felony nor a misdemeanor needs to have been committed in the officers’ presence to support a warrantless arrest
original_rationale: Atwater addresses warrantless arrests for minor misdemeanors under the Fourth Amendment, not a circuit-court consensus on whether offenses must be committed in the officer's presence.
new_rationale: Atwater addresses warrantless arrests for minor offenses committed in the officer's presence and reaffirms the in-presence requirement; it does not hold that misdemeanors need not occur in the officer's presence.

matched_cl_name: Old Chief v. United States
gold_cite: United States v. Han, 962 F.3d 568 (2020)
proposition: appropriate safeguards to minimize the danger of unfair prejudice include a limit on what details the Government can elicit and a precautionary limiting instruction

new_rationale: Old Chief addresses Rule 403 stipulation/admission of prior-conviction status, not safeguards like limiting Government details elicited or precautionary limiting instructions to minimize unfair prejudice.

proposition: “Thus, although 4 all hazardous wastes are solid wastes, not all solid wastes are hazardous wastes.”
original_rationale: The excerpt addresses CWA and RCRA citizen suits, mootness, and standing â€” it contains no discussion of the relationship between hazardous wastes and solid wastes.

new_rationale: The opinion does not articulate any rule about declining to consider issues raised for the first time at oral argument; it addresses indictment sufficiency and duplicity.

new_rationale: This case concerns California's anti-SLAPP statute and limited-purpose public figures in defamation, not Rule 15(d) supplemental pleadings.

original_rationale: The excerpt does not mention Singletary or any specific determination required by it; the case addresses medical improvement standards and evidence consideration, not the Singletary issue.

gold_cite: Bennett v. Schmidt, 153 F.3d 516 (1998)
proposition: contrasting the standard for resolving a motion to dismiss with the standard on summary judgment
original_rationale: Anderson addresses the summary judgment standard (and its relation to directed verdict), not a contrast between motion-to-dismiss and summary-judgment standards.

gold_cite: Keeton v. Morningstar, Inc., 667 F.3d 877 (2012)

new_rationale: Scott v. Harris addresses Fourth Amendment reasonableness in police pursuits and the videotape exception to viewing facts favorably to nonmovant; it does not stand for the proposition that a fact is deemed admitted when a party fails to create a genuine dispute.

matched_cl_name: Flannery v. Allstate Insurance
gold_name: Assurance Co., R.R.G. v. First Am. Title Ins. Co.
gold_cite: Assurance Co., R.R.G. v. First Am. Title Ins. Co., 853 F.3d 876 (2017)

original_rationale: Flannery expressly rejects the estoppel rule, holding that an insurer's breach of the duty to defend does NOT preclude it from contesting indemnity coverageâ€”the opposite of the proposition.
new_rationale: Flannery rejected the minority estoppel rule and held that an insurer's breach of the duty to defend does NOT preclude it from contesting coverage/indemnity, the opposite of the proposition.

new_rationale: The case acknowledges the common-law rule that a release extinguishes the cause of action but expressly repudiates it, and does not articulate the release/covenant-not-to-sue distinction stated in the proposition.

new_rationale: The opinion does not analyze continuity of shareholders by reference to cash payment versus stock exchange; the court instead emphasized that no single de facto merger factor is dispositive and upheld successor liability despite the asset sale being funded with cash/loans.

gold_cite: Signal Funding, LLC v. Sugar Felsenthal Grais & Helsinger LLP, 136 F.4th 718 (2025)
proposition: “Where a plaintiff states a plausible claim for relief under one discernable legal theory, we start and end there.”
original_rationale: The case addresses Title VII retaliation and pleading legal theories, not the proposition that courts start and end with one discernable legal theory when a plausible claim exists.
new_rationale: Hatmaker addresses Title VII retaliation pleading and the participation/opposition clauses; it says nothing about courts confining analysis to a single discernable legal theory when a plausible claim is stated.

matched_cl_name: Jarrod Stringer v. David Whitley
gold_cite: Baysal v. Midvale Indem. Co., 78 F.4th 976 (2023)
proposition: “[W]e have held that worry and anxiety are not the kind of concrete injury essential to standing.”
original_rationale: Stringer addresses Article III standing requirements for injunctive relief based on future injury risk; the excerpt contains no holding that worry or anxiety fail to qualify as concrete injury.
new_rationale: The opinion addresses standing requirements generally but contains no holding or language stating that worry and anxiety are insufficient for concrete injury.

matched_cl_name: McKenna v. Austin
gold_cite: Pate v. City of Sesser, 393 N.E.2d 1146 (1979)
proposition: “A release extinguishes a cause of action whereas a covenant not to sue affects only the right to bring suit and not the cause of action itself.”
original_rationale: McKenna explicitly rejects the release/covenant-not-to-sue distinction as 'entirely artificial,' so it does not support the proposition.
new_rationale: The court explicitly rejects the release/covenant-not-to-sue distinction as 'entirely artificial,' directly contradicting the proposition.

matched_cl_name: Pepsi-Cola Metropolitan Bottling Company, Inc. v. Checkers, Inc.
gold_cite: Goguen v. Textron, Inc., 476 F. Supp. 2d 5 (2007)
proposition: applying Massachusetts law sand finding continuity of shareholders factor not satisfied where defendant “paid cash for the assets of [the purported predecessor entity], and thus no shares exchanged hands”
original_rationale: This case concerns piercing the corporate veil and fraudulent transfers, not successor liability or the continuity-of-shareholders factor in an asset purchase context.
new_rationale: The opinion addresses piercing the corporate veil and ch. 93A unfair practices, not successor liability or continuity-of-shareholders analysis involving a cash asset purchase.

matched_cl_name: Chrysler Credit Corp. v. Country Chrysler, Inc.
gold_name: Co. v. U.S. Env’t Prot. Agency
gold_cite: Co. v. U.S. Env’t Prot. Agency, 407 F.3d 1 (2005)
proposition: noting that “a court can sua sponte order a transfer even absent a motion from either party”
original_rationale: The opinion discusses Â§ 1404(a) transfers and loss of jurisdiction upon transfer, but the excerpt contains no statement that a court may sua sponte order transfer absent a motion.
new_rationale: The opinion addresses loss of jurisdiction after a § 1404(a) transfer and Rule 21/42(b) distinctions; it does not discuss or support sua sponte transfers absent a party's motion.

matched_cl_name: Carvalho v. Equifax Information Services, LLC
gold_name: Anthony v. GE Capital Retail Bank
gold_cite: Anthony v. GE Capital Retail Bank, 321 F. Supp. 3d 469 (2017)
proposition: finding that, unless there is a showing that a credit card issuer operated as a consumer reporting agency governed by § 1681i, a cause of action against a furnisher under § 1681i is improper
original_rationale: The excerpt addresses CAFA removal timeliness and CRA reinvestigation duties under FCRA/CCRAA, not Â§ 1681i furnisher liability or the credit card issuer/CRA distinction stated in the proposition.
new_rationale: Carvalho addresses the inaccuracy requirement for § 1681i reinvestigation claims against CRAs and FCRA preemption of state-law furnisher claims; it does not hold that § 1681i claims against furnishers fail unless the furnisher operated as a CRA.

matched_cl_name: Wong v. PartyGaming Ltd.
gold_cite: Huffington v. T.C. Grp., LLC, 637 F.3d 18 (2011)
proposition: referring to the “unreasonable and unjust” condition as “more demanding” than a mere inquiry into “contacts” or “convenience” (citing Carnival Cruise Lines, Inc. v. Shute, 499 U.S. 585, 595 (1991))
original_rationale: The excerpt cites Carnival Cruise/Shute for the proposition that a forum selection clause should be upheld absent a strong showing, not for characterizing an 'unreasonable and unjust' standard as more demanding than a contacts or convenience inquiry.
new_rationale: The excerpt cites Shute for the standard that a forum selection clause should be upheld absent a strong showing, but does not characterize the 'unreasonable and unjust' condition as 'more demanding' than inquiries into contacts or convenience.

matched_cl_name: Rolando Gus Paez v. Secretary, Florida Department of Corrections
gold_cite: Delgado v. Dennehy, 503 F. Supp. 2d 411 (2007)
proposition: “Motions to dismiss habeas petitions pursuant to Fed. R. Civ. P. 12(b)(6) are not inconsistent with habeas rules.”
original_rationale: The case addresses sua sponte Rule 4 dismissal of an untimely Â§ 2254 petition, not the consistency of Rule 12(b)(6) motions with habeas rules.
new_rationale: The case addresses sua sponte Rule 4 dismissal of an untimely §2254 petition, not whether Rule 12(b)(6) motions are consistent with habeas rules.

matched_cl_name: Richard Atchinson v. District of Columbia
gold_cite: Acción v. Hernandez, 367 F.3d 61 (2004)
proposition: a “complaint should at least set forth minimal facts as to who did what to whom, when, where, and why—although why, when why means the actor’s state of mind, can be averred generally”
original_rationale: The opinion addresses Rule 8 notice pleading and Leatherman's rejection of heightened pleading for Â§1983 municipal liability claims; it does not articulate the 'who/what/when/where/why' formulation in the proposition.
new_rationale: The opinion discusses Rule 8 notice pleading and Leatherman's rejection of heightened pleading for §1983 municipal claims, but contains no 'who, what, when, where, why' formulation or statement that state of mind may be averred generally.

matched_cl_name: Thompson v. Paul
proposition: “[Plaintiffs] waited until they lost before asking for certification. This is almost always fatal unless the court sees strong policy reasons to insist on certification itself.”
original_rationale: Thompson v. Paul concerns Section 10(b) securities fraud and certification of a state-law question to a state supreme court, not class certification, and says nothing about plaintiffs seeking class certification after losing.
new_rationale: The case explicitly establishes a presumption against certification when sought only after an adverse district court ruling, supporting the proposition that post-loss certification requests are almost always denied.

matched_cl_name: Pete Thomas v. EMC Mortgage Corporation, et
gold_cite: Koufos v. U.S. Bank, N.A., 939 F.Supp.2d 40 (2013)
proposition: continued threats to collect upon and enforce a legally enforceable debt, including threatened home foreclosure, insufficient to state a claim of IIED
original_rationale: The opinion addresses breach of contract, unreasonable collection, negligent misrepresentation, and TDCPA claims, but contains no IIED (intentional infliction of emotional distress) claim or analysis.
new_rationale: opinion fits within 20K, no new text exposed

matched_cl_name: Renchenski v. Williams
gold_cite: Dongarra v. Smith, 27 F.4th 174 (2022)
proposition: “It is obvious that branding [the prisoner plaintiff] a sex offender could make him a target of prison violence.”
original_rationale: The excerpt discusses due process and self-incrimination concerns from sex offender labeling but contains no statement that branding a prisoner a sex offender exposes him to prison violence.
new_rationale: The opinion expressly notes that sex offenders are targets of savage beatings and sexual violence in prison, directly supporting the proposition that branding an inmate a sex offender exposes him to prison violence.

matched_cl_name: Armstrong Surgical Center, Inc. v. Armstrong County Memorial Hospital
gold_name: Latson v. Plaza Home Mortg., Inc.
gold_cite: Latson v. Plaza Home Mortg., Inc., 708 F.3d 324 (2013)
proposition: rejecting tolling argument where challenged “terms” and “implications of [contractual] burdens were apparent” at signing
original_rationale: This antitrust/Noerr-Pennington immunity case has nothing to do with tolling or contractual terms being apparent at signing.
new_rationale: This case concerns Noerr-Pennington antitrust immunity for petitioning conduct, not statute of limitations tolling or contractual burdens known at signing.

matched_cl_name: Mashpee Tribal Cncl v. Norton, Gale A.
gold_name: Mashpee Wampanoag Tribal Council, Inc. v. Norton
gold_cite: Mashpee Wampanoag Tribal Council, Inc. v. Norton, 336 F.3d 1094 (2003)
proposition: “[The rule of reason] cannot be decided in the abstract, by reference to some number of months or years beyond which agency inaction is presumed to be unlawful but will depend in large part . . . upon the complexity of the task at hand, the significance (and permanence
original_rationale: The excerpt does not contain the quoted language about 'rule of reason' depending on complexity and significance; that formulation comes from In re International Chemical Workers Union, not this case.
new_rationale: The opinion expressly states the rule of reason cannot be decided in the abstract by reference to some number of months or years but depends on the complexity of the task, significance and permanence of the outcome, and agency resources.

matched_cl_name: Medtech Products Inc. v. RANIR, LLC
gold_name: Singh v. Blue Cross/Blue Shield of Massachusetts, Inc.
gold_cite: Singh v. Blue Cross/Blue Shield of Massachusetts, Inc., 308 F.3d 25 (2002)
proposition: noting that a plaintiff “may not speculate about future business relationships when alleging this tort”
original_rationale: The excerpt does not contain any statement that a plaintiff may not speculate about future business relationships when alleging tortious interference; the visible portion addresses trade secret misappropriation pleading standards.
new_rationale: The excerpt addresses tortious interference claims but contains no statement that a plaintiff may not speculate about future business relationships when alleging this tort.

matched_cl_name: Baskin-Robbins Franchising LLC v. Alpenrose Dairy, Inc.
gold_name: LaVallee v. Parrot-Ice Drink Prods. of Am., Inc.
gold_cite: LaVallee v. Parrot-Ice Drink Prods. of Am., Inc., 193 F. Supp. 2d 296 (2002)
proposition: “The transmission of information into Massachusetts by way of the mails, the internet, or the telephone is decidedly a contact for . . . the relatedness analysis.”
original_rationale: The excerpt does not contain the quoted language about transmission of information into Massachusetts being a contact for relatedness analysis.
new_rationale: The opinion does not contain the quoted language about transmissions via mail/internet/telephone being a contact for relatedness; that phrasing comes from a different case.

matched_cl_name: Platten v. HG Bermuda Exempted Ltd.
gold_cite: Lipsitt v. Plaud, 994 N.E.2d 777 (2013)
proposition: noting that the Supreme Judicial Court “recognized” these factors in Attorney General v. M.C.K., Inc., 432 Mass. 546, 555 & n.19, 736 N.E.2d 373, 380–81 & n.19 (2000)
original_rationale: The excerpt does not mention Attorney General v. M.C.K., Inc. or discuss factors recognized by the Massachusetts Supreme Judicial Court relevant to the proposition.
new_rationale: The opinion at 736 N.E.2d 373, 380 n.19 explicitly cites and recognizes the twelve enumerated factors (the 'Pepsi-Cola factors') governing corporate disregard, supporting the proposition.

matched_cl_name: Ion Media Networks, Inc. v. Cyrus Select Opportunities Master Fund, Ltd. (In Re Ion Media Networks, Inc.)
gold_name: In Bank of New England Corp.)
gold_cite: In Bank of New England Corp.), 364 F.3d 355 (2004)
proposition: interpreting debt subordination agreement and explaining that “property interests should not be analyzed differently as a result of a party’s involvement in a bankruptcy case” (citing Butner v. United States, 440 U.S. 48, 55 (1979))
original_rationale: The excerpt addresses standing under an intercreditor agreement and FCC license collateral issues; it contains no discussion of Butner v. United States or the principle that property interests are analyzed the same inside and outside bankruptcy.
new_rationale: The excerpt does not cite Butner v. United States or contain the quoted language about property interests not being analyzed differently due to bankruptcy involvement.

matched_cl_name: Commercial Union Insurance v. Seven Provinces Insurance
gold_name: Framingham Auto Sales, Inc. v. Workers’ Credit Union
gold_cite: Framingham Auto Sales, Inc. v. Workers’ Credit Union, 671 N.E.2d 963 (1996)
proposition: “[A] mere breach of a legal obligation under commercial law, without more, does not amount to an unfair and deceptive act under [Chapter 93A].” (citing Whitinsville Plaza, Inc. v. Kotseas, 390 N.E.2d 243 (Mass. 1979))
original_rationale: The excerpt is from Commercial Union v. Seven Provinces, not Whitinsville Plaza v. Kotseas, and does not contain the cited proposition about mere breach of commercial-law obligations under 93A.
new_rationale: This case is Commercial Union v. Seven Provinces, not Whitinsville Plaza v. Kotseas; while it discusses 93A and breach of contract, it is the wrong cited case for the proposition.

original_rationale: The 56 n.7 footnote about judicial notice of other court proceedings does not appear in this excerpt, which addresses ancillary jurisdiction, credibility findings, and ex parte communicationsâ€”no on-point passage supports the proposition.
